title: Feature-Market Fit: Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings (NAICS 531120)

sections.title: Input Context
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Technology class | Emergency Exit Security Devices with Remote Monitoring |
| Commodity FP | Prevent unauthorized use of emergency exit doors while maintaining unimpeded escape capability and alerting to door activation via audible alarm |
| Product FP | Secure emergency exit doors against unauthorized use while preserving unimpeded single-motion escape capability and reporting door status events to centralized monitoring — without mains power at the door |
| Market needs source | JTBD_lessors_nonresidential_buildings.md — 22 error statements (N01–N22) |
| Top underserved needs | N10 (opp 16), N05 / N13 / N17 (opp 15 each), N04 / N11 / N22 (opp 14 each) |
| Dominant alternative | A1 — mechanical-only door alarms (~40% share); A5 — manual patrol (~30%) |
| Best monitoring incumbent | A3 — dormakaba SafeRoute (wired electronic, LON/BACnet, ~25% share) |

---

sections.title: Step 09 — Technology-Level Feature-Market Fit
sections.content:
### Kano + Overall Fit Summary

| # | Feature | Category | Kano Class | Overall Fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Mechanical Handle Locking | safety | `must_be` | 5.22 |
| F2 | Audible Alarm Trigger | safety | `must_be` | 5.78 |
| F3 | Single-Motion Emergency Release | usability | `must_be` | 5.78 |
| F4 | Key Override Bypass | usability | `one_dimensional` | 5.44 |
| F5 | Battery-Powered Operation | maintenance | `must_be` | 5.44 |
| F6 | Pre-Alarm Warning | usability | `attractive` | 5.00 |

### ODI Dimension Scores (1–10 scale)

| Feature | Time | Cost | Safety | Reliability | Skill | Stress | Pain | Confidence | Delight | **Overall** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Handle Locking | 2 | 4 | 9 | 8 | 5 | 6 | 4 | 8 | 1 | **5.22** |
| Audible Alarm Trigger | 2 | 4 | 10 | 8 | 6 | 7 | 6 | 7 | 2 | **5.78** |
| Single-Motion Emergency Release | 3 | 3 | 10 | 7 | 8 | 7 | 4 | 9 | 1 | **5.78** |
| Key Override Bypass | 6 | 5 | 5 | 6 | 5 | 7 | 6 | 6 | 3 | **5.44** |
| Battery-Powered Operation | 5 | 8 | 3 | 4 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 5 | 3 | **5.44** |
| Pre-Alarm Warning | 3 | 4 | 4 | 5 | 5 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 6 | **5.00** |

*overall_fit = arithmetic mean of 9 dimension scores, rounded to 2 decimal places.*

### Rationale Per Feature

| Feature | Kano justification | Primary ODI drivers | Market evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Handle Locking** | Commodity FP core. Absence = total product failure. Presence assumed. | Safety (9): sole mechanism preventing unauthorized access; Reliability (8): spring fatigue is the primary failure mode | N04 (false-negative alarm, imp=10) depends on this mechanism not failing |
| **Audible Alarm Trigger** | Commodity FP explicitly names audible alert. No building lessor is "delighted" by it — they expect it. | Safety (10): primary deterrence + alerting; Stress (7): FM trusts notification without dashboard; Pain (6): displaces dedicated door-watcher | N04 (false-negative, imp=10); Stress (7) directly relevant for buildings without IoT monitoring |
| **Single-Motion Emergency Release** | Absolute regulatory requirement (EN 179 / EN 1125). Failure = certification withdrawal. | Safety (10): fire evacuation compliance; Confidence (9): certification provides legal certainty; Skill (8): no prior knowledge needed for transient building occupants | N07 (power-independent alarm readiness, imp=10); passes litmus for all building-type subsets |
| **Key Override Bypass** | `one_dimensional` — more granular access management (electronic log, per-door programming) proportionally improves FM operations. Not expected by default. | Stress (7): directly addresses N08 (false alarms from authorized access, imp=8, sat=5); Pain (6): eliminates alarm-trigger/silence cycle for routine maintenance | N08 (authorized access false positives, imp=8, sat=5) — improvement is linear with key management quality |
| **Battery-Powered Operation** | `must_be` for this market: existing commercial building stock makes electrical retrofit prohibitively disruptive. Without battery operation, the product cannot reach >80% of portfolio. | Cost (8): eliminates electrician labor + conduit + permits (N01, imp=8, sat=3, opp=13); Pain (8): removes wiring-project management from FM workload; Confidence (5): battery life uncertainty (C4) and N13 (opp=15) lower trust vs. wired | N01 (no permanent electrical modification, imp=8, sat=3): dominant market need this feature serves |
| **Pre-Alarm Warning** | `attractive` — dominant A1 competitor (40% share) does not have this. Absence is not a disappointment. Multi-tenant commercial buildings have diverse occupants (cleaning staff, couriers, contractors) who trigger accidental alarms regularly. | Delight (6): "warns before it alarms" is genuinely unexpected in this market; Stress (7): reduces FM anxiety about false alarms in busy buildings; Pain (6): reduces alarm-fatigue from accidental activations | N08 (false alarms from authorized access, imp=8): pre-alarm is a softer catch before key override is even needed |

---

sections.title: Step 10 — Product-Related Needs (Burleson Product Jobs)
sections.content:
Needs that exist BECAUSE of choosing this technology. Would disappear if a wired electronic system (A3) or mechanical-only system (A1) were chosen instead. Do NOT duplicate N01–N22 from JTBD analysis.

| ID | Statement | Source Constraint | Imp | Sat | Opp |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PR1 | Minimize the likelihood of EasyWave protocol dependency requiring full hardware replacement when a commercial property portfolio transitions to an open-standard BMS platform (KNX or BACnet), measured as percentage of installed door guardian units requiring hardware replacement versus gateway reconfiguration during BMS platform migration | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In (C6) | 8 | 2 | 14 |
| PR2 | Minimize the likelihood of 868 MHz signal attenuation through reinforced concrete floor slabs creating per-floor monitoring blackouts in multi-story commercial office buildings, measured as number of unplanned repeater additions required to achieve full portfolio coverage versus initial installation plan | Wireless Range Limitation (C3) | 7 | 3 | 11 |
| PR3 | Minimize the likelihood of IoT door-event timestamp collection triggering a GDPR Article 35 Data Protection Impact Assessment in EU commercial office deployments with employee-occupied areas, measured as percentage of deployments requiring DPIA completion before system activation | GDPR Monitoring Data Compliance (C8) | 7 | 3 | 11 |
| PR4 | Minimize the likelihood of unheated commercial stairwell and service corridor temperature variations reducing battery service life below the 2-year maintenance target, measured as mean observed battery replacement interval deviation for units in HVAC-dark zones versus conditioned-space units | IoT Battery Life Floor (C4) | 6 | 4 | 8 |

**Litmus-test confirmations:**
- PR1: A3 (wired/SafeRoute) uses open protocols natively → need disappears ✓
- PR2: A3 (wired) has no RF constraint → need disappears ✓
- PR3: A1 (mechanical-only) collects no data → no DPIA required → need disappears ✓
- PR4: A3 (wired/mains-powered) has no battery → need disappears ✓

---

sections.title: Step 11 — Vendor Feature-Market Fit
sections.content:
*Vendor: GfS Rettungswegtechnik / Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT*
*Best incumbent benchmark: dormakaba SafeRoute (wired electronic exit control, LON/BACnet, ~25% share in monitored exit control)*

| # | Feature | Kano | Vendor Score | Incumbent Score | Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| V1 | 868 MHz Wireless Radio Transmission | `attractive` | 7 | 4 | **superior** |
| V2 | Wireless BUS Interface Integration | `attractive` | 6 | 5 | **parity** |
| V3 | Dual-Standard Variant Range (EN 179 + EN 1125) | `attractive` | 8 | 5 | **superior** |
| V4 | IP45 Outdoor Variant | `attractive` | 6 | 3 | **superior** |
| V5 | Glass-Frame Mounting Plate | `attractive` | 7 | 2 | **superior** |

### Vendor Feature Rationale

| Feature | Vendor assessment | Incumbent comparison | Key trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **868 MHz Wireless Radio** | 868.3 MHz EasyWave, ~30 m integrated TX, extendable via repeater. Enables retrofit in occupied buildings without electrical conduit. Proprietary protocol. | dormakaba SafeRoute delivers richer monitoring via wired LON bus with BMS integration — but requires mains wiring + conduit per door, directly conflicting with N01 (imp=8, sat=3). SafeRoute cannot address the 80% of existing building stock where wiring retrofit is disruptive. | Vendor wins on deployment accessibility; loses on protocol openness (C6) and BMS integration depth |
| **Wireless BUS Interface** | GfS Wireless BUS Interface aggregates multi-unit status wirelessly into a central monitoring point. Enables portfolio-level dashboards addressing N11 (opp=14). Limited to ~50 units per gateway; EasyWave-only. | SafeRoute offers centralized PC-based monitoring software with richer reporting but requires full wired network. Per-building monitoring depth favors SafeRoute; portfolio retrofit accessibility favors GfS. Net outcome parity for medium portfolios. | Gap within measurement uncertainty (delta=1); SafeRoute better at scale, GfS better at retrofit |
| **Dual-Standard Variant Range** | Matched EN 179 (lever handle) and EN 1125 (panic bar) variants from the same product family — identical receiver, BUS interface, and installation procedure. Reduces procurement complexity for mixed commercial portfolios. | GEZE SecuLogic covers EN 179 + EN 13637 (electronic), not a matched EN 1125 panic-bar variant. dormakaba SafeRoute is electronic-first and covers EN 13637 only. No identified incumbent offers a wireless-ready matched EN 179 + EN 1125 product family from a single system. | Clear advantage for mixed-door commercial portfolios (Class A + retail + industrial flex) |
| **IP45 Outdoor Variant** | Art. 990000-IP provides light weather protection for loading dock exits, covered stairwells, and underground parking emergency exits — relevant for ~15–20% of commercial portfolio doors. Not suitable for washdown (IP65+ required). | No identified mechanical or electronic competitor publishes a specific IP-rated variant with wireless monitoring capability. Mechanical-only competitors occasionally offer weatherized housings but no wireless monitoring. | Genuine differentiator for outdoor/semi-exposed doors; not relevant for the majority of internal office exits |
| **Glass-Frame Mounting Plate** | Art. 991170 enables installation on glass-panel emergency exit doors without structural modification. Critical for Class A commercial offices with glass-partition layouts — a large and growing segment. | No identified competitor (dormakaba, GEZE, Allegion) offers an equivalent non-invasive glass-frame mounting solution. Most alternatives either require hardware replacement or cannot be installed on glass-frame doors. | Strongest competitive advantage in premium Class A office market (S5 segment) |

---

sections.title: QA Check
sections.content:
| # | Check | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | All 6 technology features assessed with Kano + 9 ODI scores | PASS |
| 2 | overall_fit computed arithmetically (sum/9 rounded to 2dp) | PASS |
| 3 | Safety ≥ 7 → Confidence ≥ 5 (internal consistency) | PASS — no violations |
| 4 | Skill ≥ 7 → Stress ≥ 5 (internal consistency) | PASS — no violations |
| 5 | Product-related needs pass litmus test (disappear with A1/A3) | PASS — all 4 confirmed |
| 6 | Product-related needs do not duplicate N01–N22 | PASS |
| 7 | Each product-related need traces to a specific constraint | PASS |
| 8 | Vendor features assessed on 0–10 scale vs. specific incumbent | PASS — dormakaba SafeRoute named |
| 9 | Advantage classification: vendor_score vs. incumbent delta > 1 for superior | PASS |
| 10 | Vendor bias check: 4/5 superior — defensible given these ARE vendor-specific differentiators | PASS — vendor features selected by GfS precisely to differentiate |

---

rows.Primary ODI drivers: Delight (6): "warns before it alarms" is genuinely unexpected in this market; Stress (7): reduces FM anxiety about false alarms in busy buildings; Pain (6): reduces alarm-fatigue from accidental activations
rows.Market evidence: N08 (false alarms from authorized access, imp=8): pre-alarm is a softer catch before key override is even needed

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of EasyWave protocol dependency requiring full hardware replacement when a commercial property portfolio transitions to an open-standard BMS platform (KNX or BACnet), measured as percentage of installed door guardian units requiring hardware replacement versus gateway reconfiguration during BMS platform migration

Minimize the likelihood of 868 MHz signal attenuation through reinforced concrete floor slabs creating per-floor monitoring blackouts in multi-story commercial office buildings, measured as number of unplanned repeater additions required to achieve full portfolio coverage versus initial installation plan

Minimize the likelihood of IoT door-event timestamp collection triggering a GDPR Article 35 Data Protection Impact Assessment in EU commercial office deployments with employee-occupied areas, measured as percentage of deployments requiring DPIA completion before system activation

Minimize the likelihood of unheated commercial stairwell and service corridor temperature variations reducing battery service life below the 2-year maintenance target, measured as mean observed battery replacement interval deviation for units in HVAC-dark zones versus conditioned-space units

rows.Vendor assessment: 868.3 MHz EasyWave, ~30 m integrated TX, extendable via repeater. Enables retrofit in occupied buildings without electrical conduit. Proprietary protocol.
rows.Incumbent comparison: dormakaba SafeRoute delivers richer monitoring via wired LON bus with BMS integration — but requires mains wiring + conduit per door, directly conflicting with N01 (imp=8, sat=3). SafeRoute cannot address the 80% of existing building stock where wiring retrofit is disruptive.
rows.Key trade-offs: Vendor wins on deployment accessibility; loses on protocol openness (C6) and BMS integration depth

rows.Vendor assessment: GfS Wireless BUS Interface aggregates multi-unit status wirelessly into a central monitoring point. Enables portfolio-level dashboards addressing N11 (opp=14). Limited to ~50 units per gateway; EasyWave-only.
rows.Incumbent comparison: SafeRoute offers centralized PC-based monitoring software with richer reporting but requires full wired network. Per-building monitoring depth favors SafeRoute; portfolio retrofit accessibility favors GfS. Net outcome parity for medium portfolios.
rows.Key trade-offs: Gap within measurement uncertainty (delta=1); SafeRoute better at scale, GfS better at retrofit

rows.Vendor assessment: Matched EN 179 (lever handle) and EN 1125 (panic bar) variants from the same product family — identical receiver, BUS interface, and installation procedure. Reduces procurement complexity for mixed commercial portfolios.
rows.Incumbent comparison: GEZE SecuLogic covers EN 179 + EN 13637 (electronic), not a matched EN 1125 panic-bar variant. dormakaba SafeRoute is electronic-first and covers EN 13637 only. No identified incumbent offers a wireless-ready matched EN 179 + EN 1125 product family from a single system.
rows.Key trade-offs: Clear advantage for mixed-door commercial portfolios (Class A + retail + industrial flex)

rows.Vendor assessment: Art. 990000-IP provides light weather protection for loading dock exits, covered stairwells, and underground parking emergency exits — relevant for ~15–20% of commercial portfolio doors. Not suitable for washdown (IP65+ required).
rows.Incumbent comparison: No identified mechanical or electronic competitor publishes a specific IP-rated variant with wireless monitoring capability. Mechanical-only competitors occasionally offer weatherized housings but no wireless monitoring.
rows.Key trade-offs: Genuine differentiator for outdoor/semi-exposed doors; not relevant for the majority of internal office exits

rows.Vendor assessment: Art. 991170 enables installation on glass-panel emergency exit doors without structural modification. Critical for Class A commercial offices with glass-partition layouts — a large and growing segment.
rows.Incumbent comparison: No identified competitor (dormakaba, GEZE, Allegion) offers an equivalent non-invasive glass-frame mounting solution. Most alternatives either require hardware replacement or cannot be installed on glass-frame doors.
rows.Key trade-offs: Strongest competitive advantage in premium Class A office market (S5 segment)

rows.Check: All 6 technology features assessed with Kano + 9 ODI scores
Product-related needs pass litmus test (disappear with A1/A3)
Product-related needs do not duplicate N01–N22
Each product-related need traces to a specific constraint
Vendor features assessed on 0–10 scale vs. specific incumbent
Advantage classification: vendor_score vs. incumbent delta > 1 for superior
Vendor bias check: 4/5 superior — defensible given these ARE vendor-specific differentiators
rows.Status: PASS — vendor features selected by GfS precisely to differentiate

entities.rationale: must_be — physical handle block is the commodity FP core. Absence = total product failure. Safety (9) and Reliability (8) are primary: directly addresses N04 (false-negative alarm rate, imp=10) which requires the mechanism to reliably engage. Cost (4) reflects displacement of wired lock hardware. Delight (1) — no lessor is surprised by a handle-locking emergency exit device having a handle lock.

must_be — commodity FP explicitly names audible alarm. Safety (10): primary deterrence and immediate notification mechanism; directly serves N04 (imp=10). Stress (7): FM receives local notification even without IoT dashboard. Pain (6): displaces need for dedicated door-watcher on high-traffic exits. Confidence (7): 95 dB piezo is well-understood and trusted in life-safety contexts.

must_be — EN 179/EN 1125 certification requirement; absence is a regulatory knockout. Safety (10): fire evacuation compliance is a non-negotiable for any commercial building lessor. Confidence (9): certification provides legal certainty that reduces FM and asset manager liability exposure. Skill (8): no prior knowledge required — critical for multi-tenant buildings with diverse, transient occupant populations. Delight (1) — legally required; no delight possible.

one_dimensional — richer key management (electronic credential, per-door audit log, multi-level access) proportionally improves FM operations. More granular = proportionally less false-alarm friction. Stress (7): directly addresses N08 (false alarms from authorized access, imp=8, sat=5). Pain (6): eliminates alarm-trigger/silence cycle for routine maintenance, delivery, and FM inspections. Time (6): authorized access is faster and less disruptive with a reliable bypass.

must_be for this market — commercial lessors operate primarily existing building stock where adding electrical conduit per door is prohibitively expensive and disruptive. Cost (8) and Pain (8) are primary: directly serves N01 (no permanent electrical modification, imp=8, sat=3, opp=13). Skill (7): no electrician required, standard building staff can install. Confidence (5) and Reliability (4) are depressed: battery life uncertainty (C4) and N13 (battery depletion detection, opp=15) create unresolved monitoring gaps.

attractive — dominant A1 competitor (40% share) does not have this feature; absence does not cause dissatisfaction. Multi-tenant commercial buildings have high accidental-activation frequency from cleaning staff, couriers, and contractors. Delight (6): 'warns before it alarms' is genuinely unexpected in this market. Stress (7): reduces FM false-alarm anxiety. Pain (6): eliminates accidental full-alarm events before key-override management even becomes necessary. Partial complement to N08 (false alarms from authorized access).

entities.statement: Minimize the likelihood of EasyWave protocol dependency requiring full hardware replacement when a commercial property portfolio transitions to an open-standard BMS platform (KNX or BACnet), measured as percentage of installed door guardian units requiring hardware replacement versus gateway reconfiguration during BMS platform migration
entities.litmus_confirmed: A3 (dormakaba SafeRoute) uses LON/BACnet natively — no migration lock-in. Need disappears.

entities.statement: Minimize the likelihood of 868 MHz signal attenuation through reinforced concrete floor slabs creating per-floor monitoring blackouts in multi-story commercial office buildings, measured as number of unplanned repeater additions required to achieve full portfolio coverage versus initial installation plan
entities.litmus_confirmed: A3 (wired) has no RF constraint. A1 (mechanical) has no monitoring at all. Need disappears for both.

entities.statement: Minimize the likelihood of IoT door-event timestamp collection triggering a GDPR Article 35 Data Protection Impact Assessment in EU commercial office deployments with employee-occupied areas, measured as percentage of deployments requiring DPIA completion before system activation
entities.litmus_confirmed: A1 (mechanical-only) collects no data — zero DPIA exposure. Need disappears.

entities.statement: Minimize the likelihood of unheated commercial stairwell and service corridor temperature variations reducing battery service life below the 2-year maintenance target, measured as mean observed battery replacement interval deviation for units in HVAC-dark zones versus conditioned-space units
entities.litmus_confirmed: A3 (mains-powered) has no battery. A1 has no IoT module. Need disappears.

entities.feature_description: Transmit door guardian state and alarm events wirelessly to central receivers using 868.3 MHz EasyWave protocol without mains wiring at the door
entities.best_incumbent_technology: dormakaba SafeRoute (wired LON/BACnet electronic exit control)
entities.rationale: GfS 868.3 MHz EasyWave enables retrofit in occupied commercial buildings without electrical conduit — directly serves N01 (imp=8, sat=3, opp=13). SafeRoute delivers richer protocol support and BMS integration depth but requires mains wiring per door, conflicting with N01 and excluding >80% of existing commercial building stock. Vendor scores 7 (not 8–9) due to EasyWave proprietary lock-in (C6) and 30 m range constraint (C3) which add post-sale infrastructure cost. SafeRoute scores 4 on this feature specifically because it requires full wiring infrastructure — not because the product is poor overall.

entities.feature_description: Aggregate emergency exit monitoring status from multiple door guardian units into a centralized Master BUS for portfolio-level visibility
entities.rationale: GfS Wireless BUS Interface aggregates up to ~50 units per gateway wirelessly, addressing N11 (portfolio dashboard, imp=8, opp=14) for small-to-mid portfolios without infrastructure investment. SafeRoute offers richer PC-based centralized monitoring software with reporting and event management — but requires full wired network per building. At 20–50 door scale in retrofit scenarios, both achieve comparable monitoring aggregation outcomes via different deployment models. Net gap within measurement uncertainty (delta=1).

entities.feature_description: Cover both lever-handle (EN 179) and panic-bar (EN 1125) emergency exit doors from a single product family with shared wireless infrastructure
entities.best_incumbent_technology: GEZE SecuLogic (EN 179 + EN 13637 electronic; no matched wireless EN 1125 variant)
entities.rationale: GfS offers matched EN 179 (lever handle) and EN 1125 (panic bar) variants from the same product family — identical receiver, BUS gateway, and installation procedure. Commercial lessors managing mixed portfolios (Class A office with EN 179, retail with EN 1125, industrial flex with both) can standardize on a single procurement. GEZE SecuLogic covers EN 179 and EN 13637 electronic but not a wireless-ready EN 1125 panic-bar variant. dormakaba SafeRoute is electronic-first (EN 13637) only. No identified wireless incumbent matches this dual-standard coverage.

entities.feature_description: Operate emergency exit monitoring in outdoor and semi-exposed installations with weather protection against dust and water jets
entities.best_incumbent_technology: Generic mechanical door alarms (no IP rating published)
entities.rationale: GfS Art. 990000-IP provides IP45 protection for loading dock exits, covered parking garage stairwells, and external emergency exits — applicable to approximately 15–20% of doors in commercial property portfolios. No identified competitor publishes a specific IP-rated variant with wireless monitoring capability. Vendor scores 6 (not higher) because IP45 is inadequate for washdown environments (IP65+ needed) and does not apply to the majority of indoor office exits. Attracts interest in S6 (industrial flex/logistics park lessors) where outdoor emergency exits are more common.

entities.feature_description: Enable emergency exit device installation on glass-framed doors without structural modification using a dedicated mounting accessory
entities.best_incumbent_technology: dormakaba SafeRoute / GEZE SecuLogic (no glass-frame specific solution)
entities.rationale: GfS Art. 991170 glass-frame mounting plate enables installation on glass-panel emergency exit doors — a significant and growing portion of Class A commercial office stock (open-plan layouts, glass partitions). Installation requires no drilling or structural modification, directly satisfying N03 (retrofit without structural modification, imp=8). No identified competitor (dormakaba, GEZE, Allegion) offers an equivalent non-invasive glass-frame mounting solution for emergency exit alarm guards. Strongest competitive advantage in S5 (Class A smart-office campus) and S3 (medical office with glass partition walls) segments.
